---
title: "Ch 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)"
subtitle: "Stand on the ground, look up, and trigonometry tells you the height"
lesson-id: "cbse10.heights-and-distances"
tier: "cbse10"
ncert-class: 10
ncert-chapter: 9
ncert-chapter-title: "Some Applications of Trigonometry"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: [cbse10.trigonometry-intro]
unlocks: [cbse11.trigonometric-functions]
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state $\sin\theta, \cos\theta, \tan\theta$ for a right triangle?
   See [`cbse10.trigonometry-intro`](../08a-trigonometry-intro/index.qmd).
2. Do you remember the special-angle values at $30°, 45°, 60°$?
3. Can you draw a right triangle from a verbal description ("a tower of
   height $h$ casts a shadow of length $s$")?
4. Can you simplify $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ to $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$?

Three yeses, you are ready.
:::

## Touch

Stand outside on a sunny day. Stand a metre stick vertically. Measure the
length of its shadow. Note the time and the date.

The triangle formed by the metre stick, the shadow, and the line from the
top of the stick to the tip of the shadow — is a right
[triangle](/glossary.qmd#triangle). The [angle](/glossary.qmd#angle)
between the ground and the line to the sun is the **angle of elevation** of
the sun.

```{ojs}
viewof H = Inputs.range([2, 50], {value: 10, step: 1, label: "Object height H (m)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof angle = Inputs.range([10, 80], {value: 45, step: 5, label: "Angle of elevation θ"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 460, h = 220, padX = 30, padY = 20;
  const rad = angle * Math.PI / 180;
  const D = H / Math.tan(rad);  // ground distance
  // scale to fit
  const maxX = Math.max(D, 30);
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([0, maxX]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([0, 60]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");
  // ground
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", padX).attr("x2", w - padX)
    .attr("y1", yS(0)).attr("y2", yS(0)).attr("stroke", "#888");
  // pole
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(D)).attr("x2", xS(D))
    .attr("y1", yS(0)).attr("y2", yS(H)).attr("stroke", "#26a")
    .attr("stroke-width", 3);
  // line of sight
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(0)).attr("y1", yS(0))
    .attr("x2", xS(D)).attr("y2", yS(H)).attr("stroke", "#c33")
    .attr("stroke-dasharray", "4,3");
  // labels
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY + 14).attr("font-size", 13)
    .text(`H = ${H} m,  θ = ${angle}°,  ground distance D = H/tan θ = ${D.toFixed(2)} m`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX + 10).attr("y", yS(0) - 4).attr("font-size", 11).text("θ");
  return svg.node();
}
```

For each combination of height and angle, the ground distance changes
deterministically. **One measurement, one angle, full geometry.**

## Wonder

1. If the angle of elevation is $45°$, what is the relationship between the
   height and the ground distance?
2. As the angle of elevation grows from $30°$ to $60°$, does the ground
   distance get bigger or smaller (with the same height)? Why?
3. What is the **angle of depression**, and how is it related to angle of
   elevation?
4. If you measured a shadow but did not know the angle of the sun, could
   you still find the height of an object by comparing it to a stick of
   known height nearby?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Equal — $\tan 45° = 1$. (2) Smaller — $\tan$ grows with the angle so
$D = H/\tan\theta$ shrinks. (3) Equal — they are alternate angles when
sight is from the higher point. (4) Yes — that is the *similar-triangle*
shortcut from Ch 6 (P2 there).
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — A tower
The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point on the ground
$30$ m away from the foot is $30°$. Find the height of the tower.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\tan 30° = H / 30$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$H = 30 \tan 30° = 30 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 10\sqrt{3} \approx 17.32$
m.
</details>

### Problem 2 — A taller tower
A tower stands vertically. From a point $20$ m from its foot, the angle of
elevation of the top is $60°$. Find the height.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$H = 20 \tan 60° = 20 \sqrt{3} \approx 34.64$ m.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Shadows at two times
A vertical pole has shadow lengths $L_1$ at angle $\theta_1$ and $L_2$ at
angle $\theta_2$. If $\theta_1 = 60°$ and $\theta_2 = 30°$, what is the
ratio $L_2 : L_1$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$L = H / \tan\theta = H \cot\theta$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$L_1 = H \cot 60° = H/\sqrt{3}$.
$L_2 = H \cot 30° = H\sqrt{3}$.
Ratio $L_2 : L_1 = \sqrt{3} : 1/\sqrt{3} = 3 : 1$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Two angles, one tower
From two points on the ground, on the same side and in line with the foot
of a tower, the angles of elevation of the top are $30°$ and $60°$. The
two points are $40$ m apart. The closer point has elevation $60°$. Find
the height of the tower.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let height = $H$, distance from closer point to base = $x$. Then $\tan 60°
= H/x$ and $\tan 30° = H/(x + 40)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$x = H/\sqrt{3}$ from the first. Substitute into the second.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{H}{H/\sqrt{3} + 40}$
$\Rightarrow H/\sqrt{3} + 40 = H\sqrt{3}$
$\Rightarrow 40 = H(\sqrt{3} - 1/\sqrt{3}) = H \cdot \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow H = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.64$ m.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Angle of depression
From the top of a 50 m lighthouse, the angle of depression of a boat in the
sea is $30°$. How far is the boat from the foot of the lighthouse?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Angle of depression" from the top equals "angle of elevation" from the
boat (alternate interior angles, with horizontal at top parallel to ground).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan 30° = 50 / D \Rightarrow D = 50 / \tan 30° = 50\sqrt{3} \approx
86.60$ m.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Two ships
A man on a cliff of height $100$ m sees two boats in the sea, on the same
side. Their angles of depression are $30°$ and $45°$. Find the distance
between the two boats.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$D_1$ at $45°$: $\tan 45° = 100/D_1 \Rightarrow D_1 = 100$.
$D_2$ at $30°$: $\tan 30° = 100/D_2 \Rightarrow D_2 = 100\sqrt{3}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Distance = $D_2 - D_1 = 100\sqrt{3} - 100 = 100(\sqrt{3} - 1) \approx
73.21$ m.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Eratosthenes measures the Earth (~240 BCE).** A Greek librarian at
Alexandria heard that at noon on the summer solstice in Syene (modern
Aswan), the sun was directly overhead — a vertical stick cast no
shadow. On the same day in Alexandria, $\sim 800$ km north, a vertical
stick cast a shadow at an angle of about $7.2°$ — a $50$th of a full
circle. The sun's rays are effectively parallel, so $7.2°$ must be the
arc on Earth's surface between the two cities. Therefore Earth's
circumference is roughly $50 \times 800 \approx 40{,}000$ km — within
$2\%$ of the modern value. One stick, one angle, one planet measured.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
The recipe is always the same: **sketch first, identify the right
triangle, write $\tan\theta = \text{vertical} / \text{horizontal}$, then
substitute special-angle values**. Two angles in the diagram means two
equations; two unknowns become solvable. The mathematics is trivial; the
mistake is almost always the sketch.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Angle of elevation.** The angle between the horizontal ground and your
line of sight up to a higher object.

**Angle of depression.** The angle between the horizontal at your level
and your line of sight down to a lower object.

**Solving height-and-distance problems** is a four-step recipe:

1. Sketch the situation as a right triangle (or two).
2. Identify which side is the height, which is the ground distance, and
   which angles are known.
3. Use the appropriate trig ratio: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{vertical}}
   {\text{horizontal}}$ is the most common.
4. Substitute special-angle values from the table; simplify.

A key fact: angle of depression from a high point equals angle of
elevation from the corresponding low point (alternate interior angles
between the two horizontals).

:::

A verse:

> *Look up — that's elevation. Look down — depression's the name.*
> *Tan of theta is height over ground; the rest is the same game.*
> *Sketch first, label twice, then trigonometry takes the lead;*
> *Substitute special angles — and the answer is your reward indeed.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A boy is standing on the ground and flying a kite with a string of length
> $100$ m at an angle of elevation of $60°$. Another boy is standing on the
> roof of a 10 m high building, and is flying his kite at an elevation of
> $45°$. Both boys are on the same side of their kites. If the kites
> coincide (are at the same point in the sky), find the length of the
> second boy's string.

For the first boy: kite height above the ground = $100 \sin 60° = 50\sqrt{3}$
m. Horizontal distance from boy to point below kite = $100 \cos 60° = 50$
m.

The kite is at the same point for both. The second boy is $10$ m up.

Vertical from second boy to kite: $50\sqrt{3} - 10$ m.
At elevation $45°$, vertical = horizontal, so the second boy's string makes
a $45°-45°-90°$ triangle with vertical leg $50\sqrt{3} - 10$.

String length = $(50\sqrt{3} - 10) \cdot \sqrt{2} = 10\sqrt{2}(5\sqrt{3} -
1)$ m.

Numerically: $\approx 10 \cdot 1.414 \cdot (8.660 - 1) = 14.14 \cdot 7.66
\approx 108.3$ m.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A pole 6 m high casts a shadow $2\sqrt{3}$ m long. Find the angle
of elevation of the sun.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan\theta = 6/(2\sqrt{3}) = \sqrt{3}$, so $\theta = 60°$.
</details>

**W2.** From the top of a building, the angle of depression of a car on
the ground is $30°$. The car is $30\sqrt{3}$ m from the foot. Find the
building's height.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan 30° = H / (30\sqrt{3}) \Rightarrow H = 30\sqrt{3}/\sqrt{3} = 30$ m.
</details>

**W3.** A ladder $10$ m long leans against a wall, making an angle of
$60°$ with the ground. How high up the wall does it reach?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin 60° = H/10 \Rightarrow H = 5\sqrt{3} \approx 8.66$ m.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** From the top of a 50 m high tower, the angles of depression of
two cars on the ground (on the same side and in line) are $45°$ and $60°$.
Find the distance between the two cars.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$D_1$ at depression $60°$: closer car. $D_2$ at $45°$: farther.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$D_1 = 50 / \tan 60° = 50/\sqrt{3}$. $D_2 = 50 / \tan 45° = 50$.
Distance between $= D_2 - D_1 = 50 - 50/\sqrt{3} = 50(1 - 1/\sqrt{3}) =
50 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3} - 1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{50(\sqrt{3} - 1)}{\sqrt{3}}
\cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{50\sqrt{3}(\sqrt{3} - 1)}{3} =
\dfrac{50(3 - \sqrt{3})}{3} \approx 21.13$ m.
</details>

**P2.** A vertical tower stands on a horizontal plane and is surmounted by
a vertical flagstaff of height $h$. At a point on the plane, the angles of
elevation of the bottom and top of the flagstaff are $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
Show that the height of the tower is $\dfrac{h \tan\alpha}{\tan\beta -
\tan\alpha}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let tower height = $H$, distance from observer = $x$. Then $\tan\alpha =
H/x$ and $\tan\beta = (H + h)/x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = H/\tan\alpha$. Substitute: $\tan\beta = (H + h) \tan\alpha / H$.
$H \tan\beta = H \tan\alpha + h \tan\alpha$.
$H(\tan\beta - \tan\alpha) = h \tan\alpha$.
$H = \dfrac{h \tan\alpha}{\tan\beta - \tan\alpha}$. $\square$
</details>

**P3.** The angle of elevation of a cloud from a point $60$ m above the
surface of a lake is $30°$, and the angle of depression of its reflection
in the lake is $60°$. Find the height of the cloud above the lake.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Reflection is at depth equal to the cloud's height *below* the lake.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let cloud height above lake = $h$, horizontal distance = $x$. Observer is
$60$ m above lake. Vertical distance from observer to cloud = $h - 60$;
to reflection = $h + 60$.
$\tan 30° = (h - 60)/x \Rightarrow x = (h - 60)\sqrt{3}$.
$\tan 60° = (h + 60)/x \Rightarrow x = (h + 60)/\sqrt{3}$.
Equate: $(h - 60)\sqrt{3} = (h + 60)/\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow 3(h - 60) =
h + 60 \Rightarrow 2h = 240 \Rightarrow h = 120$ m.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point on the
ground is $30°$. On walking $40$ m towards the tower, the angle becomes
$60°$. Find the height of the tower.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two unknowns (height, original distance), two equations.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $H$ = height, $x$ = distance after walking 40 m.
$\tan 60° = H/x \Rightarrow x = H/\sqrt{3}$.
$\tan 30° = H/(x + 40) \Rightarrow x + 40 = H\sqrt{3}$.
$H/\sqrt{3} + 40 = H\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow 40 = H\sqrt{3} - H/\sqrt{3} =
H(2/\sqrt{3})$.
$H = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.64$ m.
</details>

**S2.** A bird sits on the top of a tree 80 m high. From a point on the
ground, the angle of elevation of the bird is $45°$. The bird flies away
horizontally and after $2$ seconds, the angle becomes $30°$. Find the
speed of the bird in m/s.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
At elevation $45°$: ground distance = 80 m. At elevation $30°$: ground
distance = $80\sqrt{3}$ m.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Distance flown = $80\sqrt{3} - 80 = 80(\sqrt{3} - 1) \approx 58.56$ m
in 2 seconds. Speed $\approx 29.28$ m/s. Exact: $40(\sqrt{3} - 1)$ m/s.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**Q1 (1 mark — MCQ).** The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from
a point on the ground at a distance $30$ m is $60°$. The height of the
tower is:
(a) $30$ m  (b) $30\sqrt{3}$ m  (c) $\dfrac{30}{\sqrt{3}}$ m  (d) $60$ m.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$H = 30 \tan 60° = 30\sqrt{3}$. **(b).**
</details>

**Q2 (1 mark — fill-in).** If the shadow of a vertical pole is $\sqrt{3}$
times its height, the angle of elevation of the sun is ____.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan\theta = H / (H\sqrt{3}) = 1/\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta = 30°$.
</details>

**Q3 (2 marks).** From a point on the ground, the angles of elevation of
the bottom and the top of a transmission tower fixed at the top of a 20 m
high building are $45°$ and $60°$ respectively. Find the height of the
tower.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Distance from observer to base = $20 / \tan 45° = 20$ m. Then total height
to top of tower = $20 \tan 60° = 20\sqrt{3}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Tower height = $20\sqrt{3} - 20 = 20(\sqrt{3} - 1) \approx 14.64$ m.
</details>

**Q4 (2 marks).** The angle of elevation of the top of a hill from the foot
of a tower is $60°$ and the angle of elevation of the top of the tower
from the foot of the hill is $30°$. If the tower is $50$ m high, find the
height of the hill.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Distance between foot of tower and foot of hill = $D$. From foot of hill:
$\tan 30° = 50/D \Rightarrow D = 50\sqrt{3}$. From foot of tower: $\tan 60°
= H/D \Rightarrow H = D\sqrt{3}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$D = 50\sqrt{3}$. $H = 50\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3} = 150$ m. **Hill: 150 m.**
</details>

**Q5 (3 marks).** As observed from the top of a 75 m lighthouse, the angles
of depression of two ships are $30°$ and $45°$. If one ship is exactly
behind the other (on the same side of the lighthouse), find the distance
between them.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$D_1$ at $45°$: $D_1 = 75$. $D_2$ at $30°$: $D_2 = 75\sqrt{3}$.
Distance = $D_2 - D_1 = 75(\sqrt{3} - 1) \approx 54.9$ m.
</details>

**Q6 (3 marks).** A man on the deck of a ship 16 m above sea level observes
that the angle of elevation of the top of a cliff is $45°$. The angle of
depression of the base of the cliff is $30°$. Find the height of the cliff
and the distance from the ship.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Distance to base of cliff = $16 / \tan 30° = 16\sqrt{3}$. Cliff height
above deck = $16\sqrt{3} \cdot \tan 45° = 16\sqrt{3}$. Total cliff height =
deck height + above-deck = $16 + 16\sqrt{3}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Distance from ship: $16\sqrt{3} \approx 27.71$ m.
Cliff height: $16(1 + \sqrt{3}) \approx 43.71$ m.**
</details>

**Q7 (5 marks — long answer).** A person standing on the bank of a river
observes that the angle of elevation of the top of a tree on the opposite
bank is $60°$. When he moves $40$ m away from the bank, he finds the angle
of elevation to be $30°$. Find:
(a) the height of the tree, and
(b) the width of the river.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let tree height = $H$, river width = $W$. Two equations.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At first position (on bank): $\tan 60° = H/W \Rightarrow H = W\sqrt{3}$.
After walking 40 m back: $\tan 30° = H/(W + 40) \Rightarrow H = (W + 40)/
\sqrt{3}$.
Equate: $W\sqrt{3} = (W + 40)/\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow 3W = W + 40 \Rightarrow
2W = 40 \Rightarrow W = 20$ m.
$H = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.64$ m.

**(a) Height of tree: $20\sqrt{3}$ m. (b) Width of river: $20$ m.**
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar 9.3 Q1–Q12 cover single-triangle
height/distance problems like Q3 and Q4; Exemplar 9.4 Q1–Q12 are full
two-angle problems like Q5–Q7.

**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q33 mirrors Q7 (river crossing).
CBSE 2023 Standard, Q35 mirrors Q6 (ship-and-cliff).
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Angle of depression is measured from vertical."** No — it is measured
   from the **horizontal** at the higher point, looking *down*. So it
   equals (alternate-interior) the angle of elevation from the lower point.
2. **"$\tan 30° = \sqrt{3}$."** No: $\tan 30° = 1/\sqrt{3}$. $\tan 60° =
   \sqrt{3}$. Memorise the table; one swap-mistake costs ₹2-5.
3. **"All ground distances are positive, so I can drop the absolute
   values."** True for the magnitude. But signed distance matters when
   you compute "distance between two points on the same side" vs "on
   opposite sides" — sketch carefully before solving.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Sketch the triangle first.** Every wrong answer in this chapter
   begins with a sketch error, not an arithmetic one.
2. **Angle of depression equals angle of elevation across the
   horizontal.** This single fact converts every "looking down" problem
   into a "looking up" problem.
3. **Two angles, two equations, two unknowns.** When the problem gives
   two viewpoints, the height-and-distance algebra is forced.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you solve a two-angle problem (Q4, Q5, Q7) without notes,
   sketching the figure first?
2. Did Q3 and Q6 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
3. Can you recognise when a problem mixes elevation and depression
   (observer at height, target on ground or vice versa)?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo Q5 and Q7. They're the modal board questions.
- **0–1 yes:** the mistake is almost always in the *sketch*. Re-walk the
  *Apply* worked example with paper and pencil.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse10.trigonometry-intro`](../08a-trigonometry-intro/index.qmd) —
  trig ratios and the special-angle table.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse11.trigonometric-functions`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/03a-trig-functions/index.qmd) —
  the unit-circle generalisation; sum, difference, double-angle.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 10, Chapter 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Section 9.1: heights and distances.
  Common board scenarios: tower + flagstaff, ship + lighthouse, cloud +
  reflection.
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t3.trig-ratios`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/07-trig-ratios/index.qmd) —
  the ratios from a synthesis angle, with applications.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Aiming a projectile in a 3D game uses the same
right-triangle reasoning. The "lead a target" computation in
first-person shooters: predict where the target will be, then compute
the angle of elevation to send the projectile there.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Surveying — measuring real-world land — uses these exact
techniques. The Great Trigonometric Survey of India (1802–1871) measured
the height of Mount Everest, then named "Peak XV", using
height-and-distance problems exactly like Q4 and Q7, scaled to
hundreds of kilometres.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Better Explained (the *Touch* shadow narrative);
  Russian school (problem-first); Bhāskara (compact recipe).
- **Source notes:** Q7 (river crossing) is the NCERT Example 5.5
  archetype; Q5 (lighthouse) is NCERT Example 5.7.
:::
